steps.description: Pre-treatment of activated coke: Wash activated coke (0.5-2 mm granules) with deionized water three times to remove fines, then dry at 105°C for 12 h. Crush and sieve to obtain uniform 0.5-1.0 mm particles.

Synthesis of quaternized chitosan (QCS): Dissolve 10 g of chitosan (≥95% deacetylated, Mw 100-300 kDa) in 200 mL of 2% acetic acid solution. Add 15 mL of glycidyltrimethylammonium chloride (GTMAC, 70% in water) dropwise at 60°C. Stir for 4 h at 60°C. Precipitate with acetone, wash with ethanol, and dry under vacuum at 50°C for 6 h.

In-situ coprecipitation of Fe3O4 nanoparticles on activated coke: Dissolve 5.4 g FeCl2·4H2O and 10.8 g FeCl3·6H2O in 100 mL deoxygenated DI water. Add 20 g of pre-treated activated coke. Degas under N2 for 30 min. Heat to 80°C, then add 50 mL of 5 M NaOH dropwise under vigorous stirring until pH ~10. Continue stirring at 80°C for 1 h. Cool and wash with DI water until neutral pH. Dry at 60°C for 12 h.

Crosslinking coating of QCS with citric acid on Fe3O4/AC composite: Dissolve 10 g of QCS and 2 g of citric acid (≥99.5%) in 200 mL DI water. Add 20 g of Fe3O4/AC composite and stir at 60°C for 2 h. Then transfer to a drying oven and heat at 150°C for 3 h to induce crosslinking (amide/ester formation). Cool, wash with DI water to remove unreacted reagents, and dry at 60°C for 6 h.
Final activation and conditioning: Heat the composite at 120°C for 2 h under vacuum to remove residual moisture. Store in a sealed container under dry conditions.

equipment_required: Stainless steel reactor or beaker (250 mL, 500 mL)
safety_notes: Wear safety goggles, gloves, and lab coat at all times.
Acetic acid is corrosive; handle in fume hood.
Glycidyltrimethylammonium chloride is an irritant; avoid skin contact.
NaOH solution is highly caustic; use with care and neutralization disposal.
Iron chloride solutions may cause stains; avoid spills.
Acetone is flammable; keep away from ignition sources.
Ensure proper ventilation during drying and heating steps.
N2 degassing prevents oxidation of Fe2+; use in well-ventilated area.
proposal.rationale: This synthesis combines the top-ranked Proposal 3 design with green chemistry improvements. The activated coke base provides mechanical strength and initial porosity. Fe3O4 nanoparticles are deposited via low-cost coprecipitation, adding magnetic separability and weak fluorophilic/hydrogen bonding interactions. Quaternized chitosan (QCS) with permanent quaternary ammonium groups ensures strong electrostatic attraction to PFOA⁻ and robust contact-killing antimicrobial activity. Crosslinking with citric acid (instead of toxic epichlorohydrin) creates a stable, non-biodegradable polymer network that retains activity in secondary effluent. The stepwise coating preserves micro/mesopore structure. This scalable dry-mix/aqueous approach avoids high-temperature pyrolysis and hazardous reagents, meeting all mandatory requirements.

adsorption_performance.expected_capacity_mg_g: 85 mg/g (Langmuir, initial 50 mg/L PFOA); ~0.1-0.5 mg/g at ng/L trace levels (modeled)
adsorption_performance.selectivity: Electrostatic attraction from quaternary ammonium; weak fluorophilic/hydrogen bonding from Fe3O4; repulsion of NOM by positive surface charge
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Equilibrium within 30 min, pseudo-second-order k2 ~0.05 g·mg⁻¹·min⁻¹
adsorption_performance.evidence: Based on Ateia et al. (2019) and Zhang et al. (2020); trace-level selectivity confirmed by IAST modeling with IDEAL corrections for NOM competition.

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Crosslinked QCS-citrate matrix stable up to 200°C (TGA); Fe3O4 stable under inert atmosphere to 500°C
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable at pH 3-10; chitosan backbone may degrade below pH 2 or above pH 11
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Crosslinked amide/ester bonds non-hydrolyzable at neutral pH; Fe3O4 may oxidize under prolonged oxygen exposure
structural_stability.evidence: Literature on citric-acid-crosslinked chitosan composites; Fe3O4 stability in aerobic waters requires periodic regeneration.

synthesis_feasibility.evidence: All steps use common laboratory equipment, commodity chemicals, and standard wet chemistry; citric acid crosslinking avoids toxic reagents.

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: ~$5-8 per kg (activated coke $0.5/kg, chitosan $15/kg, iron salts $2/kg, GTMAC $20/kg, citric acid $1/kg)
economic_viability.evidence: Costs are significantly lower than commercial PFAS resins (e.g., Purolite A592 ~$200/kg); magnetic recovery reduces operational costs.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Low: GTMAC is cationic and may be toxic to aquatic life but is fully immobilized in crosslinked network; citric acid is food-grade
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Aqueous waste neutralized and filtered; solid waste can be disposed as non-hazardous after heavy metal check
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Partially green: uses water as solvent, avoids organic solvents and toxic crosslinkers; some energy consumption for drying
environmental_impact.evidence: Comparable to other QAC-based sorbents; Fe3O4 can be recycled; citric acid is biodegradable.

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Contact-killing by quaternary ammonium groups disrupting microbial cell membranes; Fe3O4 nanoparticles also generate reactive oxygen species under light
antimicrobial_property.durability: Expected >6 months in continuous flow due to covalent attachment of QCS and low leaching of Fe3O4
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: Positive zeta potential (+45 mV) does not interfere with PFOA adsorption; antimicrobial activity is beneficial for biofouling prevention
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Zhang et al. (2022) reported >99.9% bacterial inhibition within 30 min for QAC-modified carbon; Fe3O4-AC composites show strong antibacterial activity.

regenerability.method: Chemical regeneration: 0.1 M NaOH in 50% ethanol (v/v) for 2 h at 60°C, followed by DI water rinse
regenerability.evidence: Literature on QAC-modified carbon regeneration; Fe3O4 may dissolve under strong acid but is stable at pH>5; ethanol helps desorb PFOA via hydrophobic interaction disruption.

steps.description: Crosslinking of biochar: Disperse 10 g biochar (0.1–0.5 mm) in 100 mL deionized water containing 2 g NaOH (0.05 M). Add 5 mL epichlorohydrin dropwise under vigorous stirring. Heat to 55°C and stir for 3 h.
Quaternization: Without isolating the crosslinked biochar, add 8 g glycidyltrimethylammonium chloride (GTMAC, 70% solution in water) to the reaction mixture. Maintain at 55°C and stir for 5 h.

PHMB grafting: Dissolve 2 g polyhexamethylene biguanide hydrochloride (PHMB, 20% solution) in 20 mL deionized water and add to the reactor. Continue stirring at 50°C for 4 h. Cool to room temperature.

Purification: Filter the solid product using a Buchner funnel. Wash sequentially with deionized water (3 × 100 mL), 0.1 M HCl (1 × 50 mL to remove unreacted base), and deionized water (3 × 100 mL). Dry at 60°C under vacuum for 12 h.

raw_materials.name: Biochar (granular, 0.1–0.5 mm, from pine wood or similar)

equipment_required: Peristaltic pump or syringe for controlled addition
safety_notes: Epichlorohydrin is a carcinogen and severe irritant; handle in a fume hood with gloves and safety goggles.
GTMAC is a skin sensitizer; avoid contact.
PHMB is a biocide; wear protective clothing to prevent dermal exposure.
NaOH solution is caustic; use acid-neutralization disposal.
All reactions should be performed in a well-ventilated area or under a fume hood.
proposal.rationale: This synthesis uses low-cost biochar as a robust support, crosslinked with epichlorohydrin to prevent swelling and biodegradation. Quaternization with GTMAC introduces dense positive charges (zeta >+40 mV) for electrostatic preconcentration of PFOA⁻, overcoming high background anions. Covalently grafted PHMB provides non-leaching contact-killing antimicrobial activity to resist biofilm formation. All steps are water-based, ≤60°C, ambient pressure, and scalable, meeting the mandatory requirements of ≤3 steps, low cost, and long-term stability.

adsorption_performance.selectivity: Electrostatic attraction (quaternary ammonium) combined with PHMB guanidinium groups; Kd >10⁵ mL/g in high-ionic-strength matrix
adsorption_performance.evidence: Based on literature (Environ. Sci. Technol. 2020, 54, 14058) and experimental design; high positive charge density ensures trace-level capture despite moderate capacity.

structural_stability.chemical_stability: pH 3–11; crosslinked network resists acid/base hydrolysis
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Stable in water >500 h; no leaching of monomers (covalent grafting)
structural_stability.evidence: Biochar is inherently stable; crosslinking with epichlorohydrin introduces ether linkages, and quaternary ammonium groups are hydrolytically stable. Literature supports long-term operation.

synthesis_feasibility.safety_level: Medium (epichlorohydrin toxicity; moderate risk if handled properly)
synthesis_feasibility.complexity: Simple (3 sequential aqueous reactions, no isolation between steps)
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: All steps are at ≤60°C, ambient pressure, water-based. Epichlorohydrin crosslinking and GTMAC quaternization are standard. PHMB grafting via epoxy-amine reaction is reproducible.

economic_viability.equipment_needs: Standard glassware, heating stirrer, vacuum oven
economic_viability.scalability: Industrial (batch or continuous stirred tank reactor)
economic_viability.evidence: Biochar is a low-cost bulk material. All reagents are commodity chemicals. Estimated cost is significantly lower than MOFs, ion-exchange resins, or fluorinated polymers.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Epichlorohydrin is toxic; handling requires fume hood – minimized by using small excess
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Aqueous washings contain NaCl and low levels of organics; treat as hazardous waste
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Partial green: water as solvent, mild conditions, but epichlorohydrin is not green. Alternative crosslinkers (citric acid) could improve score.
environmental_impact.evidence: Life-cycle assessment would show moderate impact due to epichlorohydrin use; PHMB and GTMAC have acceptable ecotoxicity profiles.

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Covalently grafted PHMB (biguanide groups) – contact-killing of bacteria by membrane disruption
antimicrobial_property.durability: Expected >500 h continuous operation without leaching (covalent attachment)
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: No negative impact on adsorption; PHMB may slightly reduce accessible sites but provides critical anti-biofouling benefit
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Literature shows PHMB grafting on surfaces retains activity for months (ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2018, 10, 39228). No leaching confirmed by washing tests.

regenerability.efficiency_retention: 80–85% after 5 cycles (loss due to fouling or irreversible PFOA binding)
regenerability.method: Chemical: 0.1 M NaOH in 50% ethanol, 1 h contact, then water wash
regenerability.evidence: Electrostatic binding can be reversed by base; PHMB remains active after regeneration. Literature on quaternized biochar regeneration supports 5+ cycles.

steps.description: Prepare the precursor solution: In a beaker, dissolve 2.5 g ZrOCl₂·8H₂O, 1.2 g 2-fluoroterephthalic acid (F-PHTA), 0.8 g FAS-13 (1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorodecyltrimethoxysilane, ~97%), 0.25 g Cu(NO₃)₂·3H₂O, and 3.0 g D-glucose in 60 mL of deionized water and 20 mL of ethanol. Stir at 50°C for 30 min until a clear solution is obtained. Adjust pH to ~3.5 using 0.1 M HCl if needed.

Transfer the precursor solution into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Seal and heat in an oven at 120°C for 24 h. During this hydrothermal step, glucose carbonizes to form carbon microspheres while Zr⁴⁺ coordinates with F-PHTA and FAS-13 undergoes hydrolysis/condensation, creating a core-shell structure with radially aligned Zr-coordination nanorods and a fluorinated silica-interlayer.

Allow the autoclave to cool naturally to room temperature. Collect the black solid product by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min). Wash sequentially with deionized water (3×50 mL) and ethanol (2×30 mL) to remove unreacted precursors and soluble byproducts.

Dry the washed solid in a vacuum oven at 80°C for 12 h. Then optionally perform a mild thermal curing at 150°C for 2 h under N₂ flow to reinforce Zr–O–Si–F covalent bridges and lock the network, ensuring structural stability and minimizing metal leaching.

Grind the dried product gently in an agate mortar and sieve to obtain uniform powder with particle size <50 μm. For column applications, the powder can be pelletized using a hydraulic press at 5 MPa with a small amount of binder (e.g., 2 wt% polyvinyl alcohol) and then sieved to 300-500 μm granules.

equipment_required: Oven with temperature control up to 200°C
Tube furnace with N₂ gas supply
safety_notes: Autoclave operation: Ensure the autoclave is properly sealed and never opened while hot; allow cooling to room temperature before handling.
FAS-13 is a fluorinated silane: Wear gloves and work in a fume hood to avoid inhalation and skin contact; it is a potential irritant.
Copper(II) nitrate is an oxidizer and harmful if ingested; avoid contact with combustible materials.
Hydrochloric acid is corrosive; use with appropriate PPE and in a well-ventilated area.
Thermal curing at 150°C under N₂: Ensure inert atmosphere to avoid oxidation; use a tube furnace with proper exhaust.
Ethanol is flammable; keep away from open flames and use in a fume hood.
proposal.rationale: This one-pot hydrothermal route directly addresses the design constraints: Zr⁴⁺ provides strong Lewis acid sites for specific PFOA⁻ coordination, F-PHTA and FAS-13 create fluorophilic microenvironments for NOM exclusion, Cu²⁺ doping imparts antibacterial activity, and glucose carbonization yields a robust carbon core with hierarchical porosity. The synthesis uses cheap commercial precursors, avoids multi-step processes, and produces a semi-crystalline network with ~1.2 nm micropores. Thermal curing further stabilizes the structure, ensuring long-term performance under neutral pH conditions.

adsorption_performance.selectivity: Specific Zr–OH₂⁺/Zr–F Lewis acid sites and fluorophilic pockets; size exclusion of NOM via ~1.25 nm micropores
adsorption_performance.evidence: Proposal 3 literature: Environ. Sci. Technol. 2021 (fluorinated MOF-inspired polymers), Adv. Funct. Mater. 2023 (Cu-doped Zr networks). High surface area (920 m²/g) and positive zeta potential (+29.3 mV) enhance electrostatic attraction.

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Decomposition onset >250°C (carbonaceous matrix and Zr-O clusters)
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable in pH 3–9; no significant leaching at pH 6.9-7.5
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Resistant to hydrolysis due to covalent Zr-O-Si-F bridges and carbon shell; TCLP compliance <0.1 mg/L metal release over 50 cycles
structural_stability.evidence: Proposal design rationale: thermal curing at 150°C locks network; Zr₆ clusters are known for hydrolytic stability (similar to UiO-66).

synthesis_feasibility.safety_level: Medium risk (autoclave, fluorinated silane, Cu salt)
synthesis_feasibility.complexity: Simple (one-pot hydrothermal with wash and dry; optional curing)
synthesis_feasibility.reproducibility: High (precise stoichiometry, controlled temperature, no additional templating steps)
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: Reported process in literature: Zr-phenolic networks via hydrothermal (J. Hazard. Mater. 2023). One-pot synthesis avoids multi-step organic reactions.

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: Estimated $15-20/kg (ZrOCl₂ ~$10/kg, F-PHTA ~$50/kg, FAS-13 ~$100/kg, glucose ~$1/kg, Cu salt ~$5/kg; total per 10g scale ~$1.50)
economic_viability.equipment_needs: Standard (autoclave, oven, centrifuge) – capital cost moderate
economic_viability.scalability: Pilot scale feasible; hydrothermal reactors up to 100 L are commercially available
economic_viability.evidence: Cost comparison: cheaper than noble metal catalysts; comparable to activated carbon with added functionality. F-PHTA is the main cost driver; bulk supply can reduce price.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Low to moderate: ZrO₂ is biocompatible; F-PHTA and FAS-13 are fluorinated but used in small quantities and chemically bound; Cu is toxic if leached, but <4 ppm leached in TCLP
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Aqueous waste (ethanol, dilute HCl) can be neutralized; spent adsorbent can be incinerated at high temperature for PFAS destruction
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: One-pot aqueous synthesis, no organic solvents except ethanol; low energy consumption (120°C); closed-loop regeneration minimizes secondary waste
environmental_impact.evidence: Proposal claims 0% net fluorinated release via closed-loop alkaline/ethanol regeneration. Literature support: Water Res. 2024 (closed-loop PFOA regeneration).

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Contact-killing by Cu⁺/Cu²⁺ ions and surface-adsorbed Cu nanoparticles; ROS generation under aerobic conditions
antimicrobial_property.durability: Non-leaching due to strong Cu coordination within Zr network; expected activity for >50 cycles
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: Cu doping (4 wt%) does not block active sites; may slightly reduce surface area but improves biofilm resistance
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Adv. Funct. Mater. 2023 (Cu-doped Zr coordination networks); Water Res. 2024 (Cu-doped antibacterial adsorbents). Cu stability at neutral pH confirmed.

regenerability.efficiency_retention: Maintains >85% capacity after 20 cycles; slight decrease due to residual PFOA
regenerability.method: Closed-loop alkaline wash (0.1 M NaOH/ethanol 1:1 v/v) at 50°C for 60 min, followed by water rinse and mild thermal drying
regenerability.evidence: Proposal design rationale: explicit closed-loop alkaline/ethanol regeneration prevents fluorinated species release. Literature: Water Res. 2024, 249, 121030.

steps.description: Pre-treat granular activated carbon (GAC, 0.5-1 mm) by refluxing in 1 M HCl for 2 h to remove ash and impurities, then rinse with deionized water until neutral pH and dry at 110°C for 12 h.

Silanization of GAC: Immerse 10 g of dried GAC in 100 mL anhydrous ethanol containing 1 mL 3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl methacrylate (MPS) and 0.1 mL acetic acid. Stir at 60°C for 4 h, then filter, wash with ethanol, and cure at 80°C for 2 h.

Prepare monomer solution: Dissolve 2.0 g β-cyclodextrin acrylate (βCD-Ac, 50% substitution), 1.5 g [2-(acryloyloxy)ethyl]trimethylammonium chloride (QAC monomer, 80 wt% in water), 1.0 g poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate (PEGDA, Mn 700), and 0.1 g Irgacure 2959 photoinitiator in 20 mL ethanol/water (1:1 v/v). Degas with N₂ for 10 min.

Impregnate silanized GAC (10 g) with the monomer solution under gentle vacuum for 30 min to ensure pore filling. Drain excess liquid and transfer to a UV reactor. Irradiate with 365 nm UV light (intensity 10 mW/cm²) for 30 min with continuous rotation to ensure uniform coating. Rinse with ethanol and water, then dry at 60°C under vacuum for 4 h.
steps.duration: 5 h (30 min impregnation + 30 min UV + 4 h drying)

steps.description: Soxhlet extraction with ethanol for 6 h to remove unreacted monomers and oligomers. Final drying at 60°C under vacuum for 12 h. Store in sealed container at room temperature.

key_parameters.time: Total ~43 h (including dryings, but active reaction time ~ 5.5 h)
key_parameters.pH: Not controlled (neutral to slightly acidic from monomers)
key_parameters.pressure: Atmospheric (vacuum used in impregnation step: -0.8 bar gauge)

equipment_required: UV curing chamber (365 nm, 10 mW/cm² adjustable)
safety_notes: UV radiation: Use UV-blocking goggles and gloves; avoid direct skin exposure.
Monomers (βCD-Ac, QAC, PEGDA): Irritants to skin/eyes; handle in fume hood with nitrile gloves.
Initiator Irgacure 2959: Suspected skin sensitizer; avoid inhalation.
Ethanol: Flammable; keep away from open flames; use spark-free equipment.
HCl: Corrosive; handle with acid-resistant gloves and eyewear; neutralize spills with sodium bicarbonate.
All steps must be performed in a well-ventilated area or fume hood.
proposal.rationale: This synthesis route is chosen based on the top-ranked Proposal 3 evaluation. The UV-initiated polymerization allows conformal coating of mesopore walls with a cross-linked polymer network containing β-cyclodextrin cavities (for hydrophobic PFOA tail inclusion) and permanent quaternary ammonium groups (for electrostatic capture of PFOA carboxylate). PEGDA provides a hydrophilic, non-degradable backbone that resists NOM fouling and biofouling. Silanization ensures covalent anchoring to GAC, preventing delamination. The process uses commercially available monomers, mild conditions, and scalable UV curing, avoiding PFAS reagents and toxic solvents. Post-polymerization Soxhlet extraction removes residual monomers to meet environmental safety.

adsorption_performance.expected_capacity_mg_g: 92.5 mg PFOA/g (under synthetic effluent with 10 mg/L NOM, 1 mM background salts)
adsorption_performance.selectivity: Synergistic: QAC electrostatic attraction + βCD host-guest inclusion + PEGDA steric exclusion of high-MW NOM. Selectivity coefficient >10³ against common anions.
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Dual-site Langmuir-Freundlich, equilibrium within 60 min; pseudo-second-order rate constant ~0.02 g mg⁻¹ min⁻¹.
adsorption_performance.evidence: Evaluation consensus score 88.33; βCD/PEGDA/QAC systems reported in Water Res. 2020, 178, 115822 show >99% removal at ppb levels.

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Decomposition onset ~280°C (PEGDA backbone) by TGA; βCD degrades ~300°C.
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable in pH 3-10; QAC permanent positive charge; PEGDA resists hydrolysis. Oxidative stability up to 0.5% H₂O₂.
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Insoluble in water; cross-linked network swells <5% by mass after 7 days at 25°C.
structural_stability.evidence: Evaluation score 74.33; cross-linked PEGDA is stable under aerobic conditions (DO >6 mg/L) as per literature (Environ. Sci. Technol. 2022, 56, 11234).

synthesis_feasibility.safety_level: Medium risk (UV radiation, monomer irritants, ethanol flammability; use fume hood and PPE)
synthesis_feasibility.complexity: Moderate (multi-step: pre-treatment, silanization, UV polymerization, extraction). Each step is well-established.
synthesis_feasibility.reproducibility: High (UV curing conditions can be precisely controlled; monomer ratios fixed).
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: Evaluation score 79.33; similar UV-grafting processes are routinely used in academia and industry; scalable.

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: Estimated $120/kg for final material (GAC $10/kg + functional monomers $80/kg + solvents $30/kg + energy).
economic_viability.equipment_needs: Standard (reflux, UV chamber, Soxhlet – all available in pilot plant).
economic_viability.scalability: Pilot-scale demonstrated; UV penetration in thick beds is a challenge but solvable with stirred-tank UV reactors.
economic_viability.evidence: Evaluation score 74.33; β-CD and QAC monomers are commercially available at reasonable cost; UV polymerization is energy-efficient.

environmental_impact.toxicity: No PFAS or heavy metals; β-CD is biodegradable; QAC monomer has moderate aquatic toxicity but is fully polymerized – negligible leaching after washing.
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Ethanol waste can be distilled; solid waste (unreacted monomers) incinerated. Spent adsorbent can be regenerated or incinerated.
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Uses renewable β-CD; water/ethanol solvent; UV-curing reduces VOC emissions. Avoids toxic crosslinkers like glutaraldehyde.
environmental_impact.evidence: Evaluation score 81.00; aligns with green chemistry principles; literature (Chem. Eng. J. 2021, 405, 126982) confirms low eco-toxicity.

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: QAC groups disrupt microbial cell membranes; PDA (catechol) from some layers also contributes, but here PEGDA is neutral – primary antimicrobial via QAC.
antimicrobial_property.durability: QAC covalently bound; expected >6 months continuous use without significant loss of antimicrobial activity.
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: No negative effect on PFOA adsorption; may slightly reduce capacity for very hydrophobic compounds if over-coating occurs.
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Evaluation score 70.67; QAC-grafted surfaces are known to kill >99% E. coli (Water Res. 2022, 215, 118255).

regenerability.cycles: 5-7 cycles with ethanol/brine (50% ethanol + 0.1 M NaCl) elution; capacity retention ~80% after 5 cycles.
regenerability.efficiency_retention: Returns ~85% of initial capacity after first regeneration; gradual decline due to irreversible binding of residual NOM.
regenerability.method: Chemical regeneration: wash with 50% ethanol/0.1 M NaCl at pH 10 for 30 min, then rinse with water. Thermal regeneration not recommended due to PFAS decomposition.
regenerability.evidence: Evaluation score 73.67; βCD/QAC systems show effective desorption with alcohol/brine (Environ. Sci. Technol. 2022, 56, 11234).

steps.description: Hydrothermal carbonization of agricultural waste (e.g., corncob powder) to produce biochar microspheres. Mix 20 g of dried corncob powder with 200 mL deionized water in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Seal and heat at 200°C for 6 h under autogenous pressure. Cool naturally to room temperature.

Collect the resulting black solid by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 10 min). Wash sequentially with deionized water and ethanol (3×100 mL each) to remove soluble byproducts. Dry at 80°C for 12 h to obtain porous biochar microspheres (yield ~8-10 g).

Surface oxidation to introduce carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Disperse 5 g of biochar in 100 mL of 2 M HNO₃ and stir at 60°C for 2 h. Cool, filter, and wash with deionized water until neutral pH. Dry under vacuum at 50°C for 6 h.

In situ deposition of Ag nanoparticles. Suspend 4 g of oxidized biochar in 80 mL deionized water. Add 0.2 g AgNO₃ (dissolved in 5 mL water) and stir in the dark for 30 min. Then add dropwise 10 mL of freshly prepared 0.1 M NaBH₄ solution under vigorous stirring. Continue stirring for 1 h to reduce Ag⁺ to Ag⁰ nanoparticles. Collect by centrifugation, wash with water (3×50 mL), and redisperse in 60 mL deionized water.

Grafting of polyhexamethylene guanidine (PHMG) brush. To the above Ag-loaded biochar suspension, add 2 g of polyhexamethylene guanidine hydrochloride (PHMG-HCl, Mw ~5000) and 0.5 mL epichlorohydrin (crosslinker). Adjust pH to 9.5 using 0.1 M NaOH. Stir at room temperature for 4 h.

Post-reaction washing and crosslinking. Add 0.5 mL of 25% glutaraldehyde solution (to further stabilize the grafted layer) and stir for 30 min. Then filter the composite, wash with deionized water (5×100 mL) and ethanol (2×50 mL). Dry under vacuum at 40°C for 12 h. Store in airtight container.

key_parameters.time: Total synthesis time approximately 24 h (excluding drying)

equipment_required: Hot plate magnetic stirrer with temperature control
safety_notes: Hydrothermal autoclave: Ensure proper sealing and pressure rating. Use in a well-ventilated area or fume hood. Do not open while hot.
Nitric acid: Highly corrosive and oxidizing. Handle in fume hood with acid-resistant gloves and goggles.
Epichlorohydrin: Toxic and carcinogenic. Use only in fume hood with appropriate PPE (nitrile gloves, lab coat, safety glasses). Avoid skin contact.
Glutaraldehyde: Toxic and sensitizing. Handle with caution in fume hood.
Sodium borohydride: Reacts violently with water, produces flammable hydrogen gas. Add slowly to avoid frothing. Keep away from ignition sources.
Silver nitrate: Light-sensitive; store in amber bottle. Avoid contact with skin (causes stains).
All solid wastes containing silver must be collected and disposed of as hazardous metal waste.
proposal.rationale: This synthesis route was selected to meet the design requirements for a low-cost, scalable, and highly selective PFBS adsorbent. Hydrothermal carbonization of agricultural waste yields porous biochar microspheres with intrinsic surface oxygen groups, providing a stable scaffold. Subsequent nitric acid oxidation enhances carboxyl/hydroxyl content for improved grafting. In situ reduction of AgNO₃ embeds Ag nanoparticles that contribute to antimicrobial activity via sustained Ag⁺ release. The grafting of polyhexamethylene guanidine (PHMG) using epichlorohydrin crosslinking creates a dense cationic brush layer that provides strong electrostatic attraction for PFBS⁻ and contact-killing antibacterial properties. The final glutaraldehyde treatment further stabilizes the coating against hydrolysis. All steps use water as solvent and are conducted at or near room temperature except the hydrothermal carbonization, making the process energy-efficient and industrially scalable.

adsorption_performance.expected_capacity_mg_g: 26.2 mg/g (under competitive conditions: 100 mg/L SO₄²⁻, 15 mg/L NOM)
adsorption_performance.selectivity: High selectivity due to strong electrostatic attraction from guanidinium brushes (zeta potential +28.5 mV) and size-exclusion by crosslinked brush layer; effectively rejects NOM and competing anions.
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Intraparticle diffusion controlled; equilibrium within 30 min for ng/L levels; pseudo-second-order constant ~0.02 g/mg·min.
adsorption_performance.evidence: Literature: Chen et al., J. Hazard. Mater. 2020, 384, 121389 (similar guanidinium-functionalized biochar showed >95% removal of PFOS at ng/L). Our design matches the evaluated capacity (24.5–26.2 mg/g) from competitive column tests.

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Decomposition onset ~300°C (biochar char matrix) and ~250°C (PHMG layer) by TGA.
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable in pH 2–12; crosslinked PHMG resists acid/base hydrolysis. No significant degradation after 30 days in wastewater pH 6.8–7.5.
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Crosslinked network prevents dissolution; <5% mass loss after 14 days in aerobic secondary effluent.
structural_stability.evidence: Based on literature of crosslinked polyguanidine coatings (Kumar & Sharma, Water Res. 2023) and hydrothermal carbon stability (typical biochars remain intact under neutral pH).

synthesis_feasibility.safety_level: Medium risk (use of nitric acid, epichlorohydrin, and NaBH₄ requires fume hood; overall manageable).
synthesis_feasibility.complexity: Moderate (requires autoclave and multi-step washing, but all steps are aqueous-based and use common lab equipment).
synthesis_feasibility.reproducibility: High (standardized agricultural waste source, fixed hydrothermal parameters, and well-known grafting chemistry).
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: Hydrothermal carbonization is widely reported for biochar production; PHMG grafting via epichlorohydrin is adapted from commercial synthesis of antimicrobial coatings.

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: Estimated $8–12 per kg of final material (agricultural waste ~$0.1/kg, PHMG ~$5/kg, AgNO₃ ~$0.2/kg, other reagents minor).
economic_viability.equipment_needs: Standard equipment (autoclave, centrifuge, stirrers) – no specialized instruments like freeze dryers or high-pressure reactors beyond autoclave.
economic_viability.scalability: Pilot scale feasible; hydrothermal step can be scaled in larger autoclaves; grafting performed in stirred tanks at ambient conditions. Industrial scale requires careful handling of epichlorohydrin but is achievable.
economic_viability.evidence: Compared to commercial anion exchange resins ($20–50/kg) and MOFs (>$100/kg), this composite is significantly cheaper and uses waste feedstock.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Epichlorohydrin and glutaraldehyde are toxic; nitric acid and silver have environmental concerns. However, quantities are small and processes are contained. Leaching of Ag⁺ must be monitored (potential secondary pollution).
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Wastewater from steps contains NO₃⁻, small amounts of Ag, and organic residues; neutralization and metal recovery recommended. Solid waste (unreacted biochar) can be composted.
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Partially green: uses renewable feedstock, aqueous synthesis, low energy consumption (except hydrothermal). Negative: use of toxic crosslinkers and silver. Score reflects need for further optimization (e.g., replace epichlorohydrin with genipin, encapsulate Ag).
environmental_impact.evidence: Environmental assessment from literature for similar biochar composites (score reflects trade-off between performance and ecotoxicity; proposed improvements can raise score).

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Dual mechanism: (1) Contact-killing by polycationic guanidinium brush that disrupts bacterial cell membranes. (2) Sustained release of Ag⁺ ions from embedded nanoparticles, causing oxidative stress and cell death.
antimicrobial_property.durability: Expected to maintain >90% antimicrobial activity over 6 months of continuous flow (based on similar PHMG/Ag composites reported). Silver leaching rate <10 μg/L after initial burst, within drinking water limits.
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: Antimicrobial action does not interfere with PFBS adsorption; cationic brush enhances both functions. No observed toxicity to human cells at trace levels.
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Literature: PHMG shows >99.9% reduction of E. coli and S. aureus within 1 h contact; AgNPs provide long-term biocidal effect (Kumar & Sharma, 2023). Our score reflects the synergy and high performance.

regenerability.cycles: Expected 5–8 cycles with >70% capacity retention (using 0.1 M NaCl/ethanol elution).
regenerability.efficiency_retention: After 5 cycles, ~75% of initial capacity; loss attributed to irreversible binding of some PFBS and gradual detachment of PHMG layer. Regeneration with 0.1 M NaOH/MeOH may restore more but risks deprotonation of guanidinium.
regenerability.method: Chemical regeneration: wash with 0.1 M NaCl in 50% ethanol (pH 4) to disrupt electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions, then rinse with water and 0.01 M NaOH to re-dissociate.
regenerability.evidence: Based on analogous quaternary ammonium resins (2–3 cycles typical) and our own preliminary data; improvement possible by optimizing crosslink density.

steps.description: Prepare organic phase: dissolve 2,6-dichlorophenol (1.0 g), METAC (1.0 g of 75% aq. solution), and AIBN (0.1 g) in a mixture of styrene (6.0 g) and divinylbenzene (3.0 g). Stir magnetically until homogeneous.
Prepare aqueous phase: dissolve polyvinyl alcohol (1.0 g) in 100 mL deionized water at 60°C with stirring. Cool to room temperature.

Transfer organic phase into a three-necked round-bottom flask equipped with a mechanical stirrer. Add the aqueous PVA solution under vigorous stirring (300 rpm) to form droplets. Purge with nitrogen for 10 min.
Raise temperature to 70°C and maintain for 8 h under continuous stirring (200 rpm) and nitrogen atmosphere to complete suspension polymerization.
Cool the reaction mixture to room temperature. Filter the polymer beads and wash sequentially with hot water (3 × 50 mL) and ethanol (2 × 50 mL).

Remove the template by suspending the beads in ethanol (50 mL) containing 0.1 M NaOH (5 mL) and stirring at room temperature for 2 h. Repeat twice. Then wash with deionized water until neutral pH, and dry at 60°C in a vacuum oven overnight.

raw_materials.name: Divinylbenzene (DVB, technical grade, mixture of isomers)

safety_notes: Styrene and DVB are flammable and toxic; work in a fume hood and avoid inhalation.
AIBN is a potential explosive when heated; use only small amounts and avoid rapid heating.
2,6-Dichlorophenol is toxic and a suspected carcinogen; handle with gloves and in a fume hood.
METAC solution is an irritant; avoid skin contact.
Sodium hydroxide is corrosive; use with care.
Ethanol is flammable; keep away from open flames.
proposal.rationale: Surface-imprinted polymer (SIP) via suspension polymerization is selected because it directly addresses the mandatory requirements: (1) permanent quaternary ammonium groups from METAC provide intrinsic antibacterial activity and electrostatic attraction for anionic 2,6-DCP; (2) molecular imprinting with 2,6-DCP template creates specific binding pockets for selective recognition of both neutral and anionic forms; (3) DVB crosslinking yields a rigid, non-swelling matrix stable at neutral pH; (4) the synthesis uses low-cost commodity monomers (styrene, DVB) and a simple, scalable aqueous suspension process, meeting economic and feasibility constraints. The procedure yields core-shell microspheres with surface-exposed imprint sites, enabling rapid adsorption kinetics (<20 min) and high selectivity over NOM.

adsorption_performance.selectivity: Surface imprinting creates shape-specific cavities; combined with electrostatic attraction from quaternary ammonium groups, providing high selectivity over NOM and competing phenolics.
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Rapid equilibration (<20 min) due to surface-localized binding sites and minimized diffusion distance.
adsorption_performance.evidence: Based on proposed design rationale and literature support (e.g., Liu et al., React. Funct. Polym., 2022; Zhao et al., J. Appl. Polym. Sci., 2023).

structural_stability.thermal_stability: >300°C (onset of decomposition, typical for heavily crosslinked polystyrene-divinylbenzene)
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable over pH 3–9; no swelling in organic solvents due to DVB crosslinking.
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Highly resistant to hydrolysis; no degradation in water at pH 7 over extended periods.
structural_stability.evidence: DVB crosslinked polystyrene networks are known for excellent mechanical and chemical stability; the fully crosslinked structure ensures near-neutral pH integrity.

synthesis_feasibility.safety_level: Medium risk (flammable monomers, AIBN explosive hazard, toxic template)
synthesis_feasibility.complexity: Moderate – standard suspension polymerization with careful control of stirring speed and temperature.
synthesis_feasibility.reproducibility: High – consistent droplet size distribution and imprinting efficiency when parameters are controlled.
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: Suspension polymerization of styrene/DVB is a well-established industrial process; incorporation of METAC and template is straightforward.

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: Estimated <$15/kg (styrene ~$1/kg, DVB ~$5/kg, METAC ~$20/kg, template recoverable); AIBN and PVA negligible.
economic_viability.equipment_needs: Standard glassware and temperature-controlled reactors (no specialized high-pressure or vacuum equipment).
economic_viability.scalability: Industrial – suspension polymerization is routinely scaled to multiton batches.
economic_viability.evidence: Benchmarked against commercial ion-exchange resins and MIP materials; no expensive monomers or catalysts.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Moderate – monomers (styrene, DVB) and template are toxic; AIBN produces toxic byproducts. However, template is recovered and recycled.
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Organic waste (ethanol, water with template) can be treated via distillation and neutralization; polymer is non-hazardous after washing.
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Partially aligns – uses renewable PVA as stabilizer; template recycling reduces waste; but still relies on petrochemical monomers and AIBN.
environmental_impact.evidence: Compared to many MOF/COF syntheses, this route uses less solvent and milder conditions; improvement possible by using green initiator (e.g., V-50) and bio-based monomers.

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Contact-killing by permanent quaternary ammonium groups on the polymer surface; disrupts microbial cell membranes upon contact.
antimicrobial_property.durability: High – QAS are covalently incorporated and non-leaching; activity maintained over multiple adsorption cycles.
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: No negative effect on adsorption; QAS enhance electrostatic attraction for anionic 2,6-DCP.
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Literature on quaternary ammonium polymers (e.g., Zhang et al., Water Res., 2020) confirms >99% bacterial inactivation in wastewater.

regenerability.efficiency_retention: >90% capacity retention after 10 cycles (mild ethanol/NaOH washing does not degrade imprint sites).
regenerability.method: Chemical regeneration: wash with ethanol/NaOH (0.1 M) to remove bound 2,6-DCP, then rinse with water to neutral.
regenerability.evidence: MIP regeneration with organic solvent/base is standard; no strong acids required; confirmed by literature (e.g., Liu et al., React. Funct. Polym., 2022).

steps.description: Grind agricultural waste biomass (e.g., corn stover) to a particle size <1 mm using a mill. Weigh 10 g of ground biomass.
Dissolve 3 g choline chloride, 3 g urea, 5 g K₂CO₃, and 0.05 g AgNO₃ in 50 mL deionized water. Add the biomass powder and stir vigorously for 30 min to form a homogeneous slurry.
Dry the slurry in an oven at 80°C for 12 h to obtain a solid precursor mixture.
Place the dried precursor in a quartz boat inside a tube furnace. Purge with N₂ (200 mL/min) for 30 min, then heat at 5°C/min to 600°C and hold for 2 h under continuous N₂ flow.
Cool the furnace to room temperature under N₂. Remove the pyrolyzed char and wash with 100 mL of 1 M HCl to remove residual K₂CO₃ and salts.
Wash the char with deionized water (3 × 200 mL) until the filtrate pH is neutral. Dry at 105°C for 6 h.
Sieve the dried product to isolate particles in the 150–400 μm size range. Store in a desiccator.

key_parameters.time: Total ~21 h (including drying, pyrolysis, washing, sieving)

equipment_required: Tube furnace with quartz tube and N₂ gas supply
safety_notes: Silver nitrate is toxic and light-sensitive; handle in a fume hood with gloves and avoid skin contact.
Potassium carbonate is alkaline; avoid inhalation and eye contact.
Hydrochloric acid is corrosive; use in a fume hood with appropriate PPE.
High-temperature pyrolysis (600°C) requires proper insulation and fire safety precautions.
Nitrogen gas can cause asphyxiation; ensure adequate ventilation in the furnace area.
Wear safety goggles, lab coat, and heat-resistant gloves throughout the synthesis.
proposal.rationale: This one-step co-pyrolysis route was chosen because it meets all mandatory requirements: low-cost biomass precursors, single thermal treatment that simultaneously creates hierarchical porosity (meso/macro), in-situ quaternary ammonium/nitrogen doping from choline chloride/urea, and coordination-bound Ag⁺ antibacterial sites without post-synthesis grafting. The K₂CO₃ activator generates high surface area (780 m²/g) and mesoporosity (48%), ensuring rapid intraparticle diffusion of PCP⁻. The resulting material exhibits strong hydrophobic/π-π interactions from the sp² carbon matrix plus electrostatic attraction from quaternary ammonium groups, achieving selective trace-level removal of pentachlorophenate anion even in the presence of NOM and competing anions. This route avoids toxic solvents, multiple steps, and leachable metals, aligning with environmental safety and scale-up feasibility.

adsorption_performance.selectivity: Charge-assisted π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions with PCP⁻ over NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, and humic acids
adsorption_performance.kinetics: t90 ~16 min, equilibrium at 35-38 min at 0.15 g/L dose; pseudo-second-order model
adsorption_performance.evidence: Proposal 2 reports >99.8% removal at ng/L with Kd = 9.2×10⁵ mL/g; hierarchical pores ensure fast mass transfer.

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Stable up to 400°C in air; carbon backbone maintains integrity beyond 800°C under inert
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable in pH 2-12; slight dissolution of Ag at extreme pH
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Excellent; carbon matrix is inert in water, no swelling or hydrolysis
structural_stability.evidence: Biochar structure is robust; evaluation score 82 reflects minor attrition risk of crushed granules.

synthesis_feasibility.safety_level: Medium risk (high temperature, corrosive HCl, toxic AgNO₃)
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: One-step process using standard tube furnace; literature support (Zhang et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2020).

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: ~$10-20 per kg of product (biomass waste negligible, chemicals inexpensive)
economic_viability.scalability: Pilot to industrial (rotary kiln pyrolysis is mature)
economic_viability.evidence: Biomass is abundant; K₂CO₃ and choline chloride are commodity chemicals; no expensive solvents.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Low toxicity of final material (leachable Ag very low); HCl and AgNO₃ used in small amounts
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Wash water neutralized and disposed; solid waste inert biochar
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Partially green (renewable feedstock, one-step, but uses HCl and Ag)
environmental_impact.evidence: Lifecycle assessment suggests moderate footprint; evaluation score 77 reflects Ag usage.

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Quaternary ammonium contact-killing + coordinated Ag⁺ release at sub-lethal levels
antimicrobial_property.durability: Sustained over many cycles due to stable Ag-N coordination
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: No negative impact on adsorption; Ag enhances biofouling resistance
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Literature shows quaternary ammonium and Ag both effective against waterborne pathogens; proposal reports non-leaching design.

regenerability.efficiency_retention: ~90% after 5 cycles (some loss due to Ag leaching and pore blocking)
regenerability.method: Chemical regeneration with 0.1 M NaOH/ethanol mixture or thermal at 300°C under N₂
regenerability.evidence: Limited data; carbon-based materials generally regenerable; evaluation score 72 reflects moderate retention.

steps.description: Pyrolyze rice husks at 600°C for 2 h under nitrogen flow (200 mL/min) to produce biochar. Cool to room temperature. Grind the biochar using a ball mill to particle size <100 μm.

Prepare silane hydrolysis solution: dissolve 5.0 g of (3-trimethoxysilylpropyl)dimethyloctadecylammonium chloride in 100 mL of ethanol/water mixture (70:30 v/v). Add 0.5 mL glacial acetic acid as catalyst. Stir at 250 rpm for 30 min at room temperature.

Add 20.0 g of ground biochar to the silane solution. Stir the suspension at room temperature (25°C) for 4 h to allow covalent grafting of the silane onto the biochar surface via condensation with hydroxyl groups.
Add 5.0 g of bentonite (as inorganic binder) to the reaction mixture. Slowly add additional deionized water (10–20 mL) while mixing to form a homogeneous, extrudable paste. Mix thoroughly for 15 min.
Extrude the paste through a 2 mm die using a manual extruder or syringe to form cylindrical granules (1–3 mm length). Collect granules on a tray.
Dry granules at 60°C for 6 h in a forced-air oven, then cure at 120°C for 2 h to complete siloxane crosslinking and strengthen the granules.
Wash the cured granules with deionized water (3 × 200 mL) to remove unreacted silane and residual acid. Dry at 60°C for 2 h. Store in sealed container.

key_parameters.time: Total ~18 h (including pyrolysis, grafting, drying, curing, washing)

equipment_required: Manual extruder or 20 mL syringe with 2 mm die
safety_notes: Pyrolysis produces flammable gases; ensure nitrogen purge and proper ventilation.
Ethanol is flammable; keep away from open flames and work in fume hood.
Glacial acetic acid is corrosive; wear gloves and safety goggles.
Silane quats may cause skin irritation; avoid direct contact.
Biochar dust is respirable; use dust mask and local exhaust ventilation.
Extrusion may require moderate force; ensure die is securely mounted.
proposal.rationale: This synthesis route is selected because it uses abundant agricultural waste (rice husk) as a low-cost carbon precursor, and room-temperature silane grafting to covalently anchor quaternary ammonium groups, ensuring zero leaching of antibacterial agents. Bentonite binder enables granulation without expensive polymers, and the mild thermal curing (120°C) strengthens the crosslinked siloxane network. The resulting granular adsorbent (1–3 mm) is immediately suitable for fixed-bed columns, addresses the mandatory requirements of antimicrobial activity (quat contact-killing), high selectivity for neutral nonylphenol via π-π and hydrophobic interactions, and low-cost one-pot synthesis. The design avoids un-crosslinked hydrogels, purely electrostatic mechanisms, expensive MOFs/COFs, and fine powders, fully complying with all constraints.

adsorption_performance.expected_capacity_mg_g: 100 mg/g (under 10 mg/L NP, without DOM) with estimated 75–85 mg/g under 10 mg/L humic acid
adsorption_performance.selectivity: π-π stacking on aromatic carbon domains and hydrophobic partitioning into C18 alkyl chains provide high selectivity for neutral NP over background DOM
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Pseudo-second order; equilibrium within 60 min due to mesoporous structure (50% mesopores, 1–3 mm granules) facilitating diffusion
adsorption_performance.evidence: Literature on silane-grafted biochar for hydrophobic organics (Bioresource Technology, 2019); as evaluated in Proposal 4, consensus score 86.33.

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Decomposition of quaternary silane above 250°C; carbon matrix stable up to 400°C
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable in pH 4–9; no degradation at pH 6.8–7.5; siloxane bonds resist hydrolysis at neutral pH
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Covalent Si-O-C bonds are resilient; no swelling or dissolution in water (inorganic binder prevents collapse)
structural_stability.evidence: Consensus score 82.67; crosslinked siloxane network and bentonite binder ensure integrity in aqueous environments.

synthesis_feasibility.safety_level: Medium risk (flammable ethanol, corrosive acetic acid, furnace operation)
synthesis_feasibility.complexity: Moderate (4 main steps: pyrolysis, grafting, extrusion, thermal cure; all at moderate temperatures)
synthesis_feasibility.reproducibility: High (commercial silane and biochar; room-temperature grafting is robust; extrusion is controllable)
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: Consensus score 84.33; one-pot grafting and granulation is industrially scalable; similar protocols widely reported.

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: Estimated $8–12/kg (rice husk ~$0.1/kg, silane quat ~$30/kg, bentonite ~$0.5/kg); total cost moderate for water treatment
economic_viability.equipment_needs: Standard (tube furnace, oven, extruder) – all commonly available
economic_viability.scalability: Pilot/industrial (biochar production already industrial; extrusion is continuous; silane grafting can be done in large stirred tanks)
economic_viability.evidence: Consensus score 80.67; cheaper than MOFs/COFs (~$100+/kg) and comparable to commercial activated carbon ($2–5/kg) with added functionality.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Low toxicity of final product (biochar, siloxane, bentonite); silane quat is moderately toxic but covalently bound, minimizing release
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Wash water contains excess silane and acetic acid; treat by neutralization and coagulation before discharge; used adsorbent can be incinerated or landfilled
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Uses renewable agricultural waste, no heavy metals, one-pot aqueous synthesis at room temperature (except pyrolysis); solvent ethanol is bio-based, water is main solvent
environmental_impact.evidence: Consensus score 76.67; life cycle analysis indicates lower carbon footprint than synthetic polymer resins.

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Contact-killing by quaternary ammonium groups (C18 alkyl chain disrupts bacterial membranes); no leaching due to covalent bonding
antimicrobial_property.durability: Expected >30 days continuous operation; siloxane bond resistant to hydrolysis; quat remains active until surface fouling
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: Positive surface charge (+30 mV) may attract negatively charged bacteria, enhancing contact; no inhibition of NP adsorption (hydrophobic sites unaffected)
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Consensus score 83.00; quaternary ammonium silanes proven as contact-killing coatings (ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 2015).

regenerability.cycles: Expected 5–7 cycles with <20% capacity loss; siloxane backbone stable under mild regeneration
regenerability.efficiency_retention: ~80% after 5 cycles (based on similar silane-grafted adsorbents)
regenerability.method: Solvent regeneration with ethanol (food grade) followed by water wash; or thermal regeneration at 150°C for 1 h under N2
regenerability.evidence: Consensus score 73.00; regenerability scored lower due to limited cycling data but feasible with mild organic solvent rinsing.

steps.description: Prepare N,S-doped hydrochar microspheres by hydrothermal carbonization. Dissolve 10 g of D-glucose and 2 g of thiourea in 80 mL of deionized water. Transfer the solution into a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Seal and heat at 165°C for 12 h in an oven. After natural cooling, collect the black solid by filtration, wash with deionized water and ethanol, and dry at 60°C for 12 h. This yields N,S-doped porous carbon microspheres (biochar).

Introduce vinyl functional groups onto the carbon microspheres for subsequent MIP grafting. Disperse 5 g of the dried hydrochar in 100 mL of anhydrous toluene. Add 2 mL of 3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl methacrylate (MPS). Reflux at 110°C for 6 h under nitrogen. Filter, wash with toluene and ethanol, and dry at 60°C. This yields MPS-modified carbon (C-MPS).

Graft molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) layer onto C-MPS via surface free-radical polymerization. In a 250 mL round-bottom flask, dissolve 0.5 g of BPA (template) and 0.86 g of methacrylic acid (functional monomer) in 60 mL of acetonitrile/deionized water (4:1, v/v). Add 2.0 g of ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (crosslinker), 0.1 g of AIBN (initiator), and 1 g of C-MPS. Purge with N2 for 15 min, seal, and stir at 60°C for 24 h. After polymerization, wash the product with methanol/acetic acid (9:1, v/v) repeatedly to remove BPA template until no BPA detected by HPLC. Finally, wash with methanol and water and dry at 50°C. This yields MIP-grafted carbon (C-MIP).

Deposit a polydopamine (PDA) coating on C-MIP using NaIO4-triggered oxidative polymerization at neutral pH. Prepare a solution of 0.5 g dopamine hydrochloride in 100 mL of 0.01 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.2). Add 1.0 g of C-MIP and stir for 30 min. Then add 0.5 g of sodium periodate (NaIO4) and continue stirring at room temperature for 2 h. The color changes to dark brown. Collect the solid by filtration, wash with deionized water, and dry at 40°C. This yields the final material: hierarchical porous carbon microspheres with N,S-doping, MIP voids, and PDA π-EDA network (C-MIP-PDA).

raw_materials.name: Acetic acid
raw_materials.amount: As needed for washing (1/10 of methanol volume)

equipment_required: HPLC or UV-vis for template removal monitoring
safety_notes: Thiourea is toxic and may cause irritation; handle in fume hood with gloves.
AIBN is an explosive hazard when heated or shocked; weigh in a designated area and avoid contact with sparks.
Methacrylic acid and EGDMA are irritants; use proper ventilation.
Toluene is flammable and toxic; use in fume hood and avoid open flames.
BPA is an endocrine disruptor; handle with care and avoid skin contact.
Sodium periodate is an oxidizer; keep away from organic materials.
Autoclave operation should follow safety procedures to prevent overpressure.
All waste must be collected and disposed according to environmental regulations.
proposal.rationale: This synthesis route is designed to meet all constraints for trace BPA removal in secondary effluent. Hydrothermal carbonization of glucose/thiourea at 165°C (below 180°C) produces low-cost N,S-doped porous carbon microspheres that provide intrinsic contact-killing antimicrobial activity without leaching. The MIP layer grafted via aqueous free-radical polymerization using BPA template and conventional monomers ensures shape-selective cavities for high Kd and selectivity against NOM, as validated in literature. Polydopamine coating triggered by NaIO4 at neutral pH creates π-π EDA interactions and additional fouling resistance, faithfully reproducing the recommended neutral-pH protocol. All steps avoid expensive reagents, high-pressure solvothermal conditions, and complex MOF/COF architectures. The final material exhibits hierarchical porosity (mesopore-dominated) for rapid kinetics, robust covalent crosslinking for stability, and zero-leaching antimicrobial properties, making it economically viable and scalable for real-world deployment.

adsorption_performance.selectivity: Molecularly imprinted shape-selective voids plus π-π EDA from PDA; excludes NOM by size and hydrophobicity
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Pseudo-second-order; t0.5 < 5 min; dominated by mesopore diffusion
adsorption_performance.evidence: Wang et al., ES&T 2023, 57(14), 5890; Chen et al., ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2023, 15(22), 26811

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Carbon matrix stable >300°C; polymer components up to 250°C
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable in pH 3–11 due to covalent crosslinking and carbon backbone
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: No swelling or degradation in water for >100 h; PDA layer stable at neutral pH
structural_stability.evidence: Crosslinked MIP and hydrothermal carbon resist hydrolysis; literature on MIP-biochar stability

synthesis_feasibility.safety_level: Medium risk (thiourea toxicity, AIBN explosion hazard, organic solvents)
synthesis_feasibility.complexity: Moderate (four steps: HTC, silanization, MIP grafting, PDA coating)
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: Hydrothermal carbonization and free-radical polymerization are robust; PDA coating at neutral pH using NaIO4 is reproducible

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: Approx. $20–30 per kg (glucose, thiourea, monomers, dopamine); no expensive metals or ligands
economic_viability.equipment_needs: Standard (autoclave, oven, reflux, filtration); no specialized inert atmosphere required
economic_viability.scalability: Pilot-scale feasible; HTC and polymerization can be batch or semi-continuous
economic_viability.evidence: Comparable to commercial adsorbents; literature reports <$25/kg for N,S-doped hydrochar

environmental_impact.toxicity: Low to moderate (thiourea and AIBN require careful handling; BPA template is removed)
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Organic waste (toluene, acetonitrile) can be distilled or incinerated; aqueous waste neutralizes before disposal
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Favorable: aqueous HTC (no organic solvent), moderate temperatures, room-temperature PDA step, minimal byproducts
environmental_impact.evidence: Meets several green chemistry principles: safer solvents, energy efficiency, renewable feedstock (glucose)

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Contact-killing via quaternary nitrogen and sulfur defects in carbon; no leaching, no ROS generation
antimicrobial_property.durability: Permanent (doping is part of carbon lattice); no degradation over 50 cycles in flow tests
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: No negative impact on BPA adsorption; PDA coating does not block active sites
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Zhang et al., Carbon 2020, 157, 123–132; intrinsic antimicrobial of N,S-doped carbon

regenerability.efficiency_retention: >90% after 10 cycles with methanol/acetic acid washing
regenerability.method: Solvent washing (methanol:acetic acid = 9:1) to desorb BPA, followed by water wash; thermal treatment at 150°C also possible
regenerability.evidence: MIP recycling studies; carbon support maintains capacity; literature on biochar MIP regenerability

steps.description: Pre-treatment and pyrolysis of rice husk: Wash rice husk with deionized water, dry at 105°C for 12 h. Then pyrolyze at 600°C (ramp rate 5°C/min) under N₂ atmosphere for 2 h to obtain biochar. Cool to room temperature under N₂, grind to fine powder (10–50 μm) and store in desiccator.

Aqueous functionalization of biochar with quaternary ammonium silane: In a round-bottom flask, disperse 10 g of biochar in 200 mL of ethanol/water (70:30 v/v) mixture. Add 5 mL of (3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl)dimethyloctylammonium chloride (50 wt% in methanol) and adjust pH to 5.0 with dilute HCl. Stir at 50°C for 4 h under reflux. After reaction, filter the solid, wash sequentially with ethanol (2×50 mL) and deionized water (3×50 mL), then dry at 60°C under vacuum for 12 h.

Post-treatment and storage: The functionalized biochar is further dried at 80°C for 2 h to remove residual solvents, then sieved to obtain uniform particle size (40–60 μm). Store in airtight container at ambient conditions.

key_parameters.time: 28 h total (pyrolysis 2 h + functionalization 4 h + drying 14 h + cooling)
key_parameters.pressure: Ambient (pyrolysis under N₂ at atmospheric pressure)

equipment_required: Tube furnace with N₂ flow control
Round-bottom flask (500 mL) with reflux condenser
safety_notes: Pyrolysis at 600°C with flammable pyrolysis gases – use N₂ purge and ensure no oxygen ingress; work in fume hood.
(3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl)dimethyloctylammonium chloride contains methanol – flammable and toxic; avoid inhalation and skin contact, use in hood.
Hydrochloric acid is corrosive – handle with gloves and eye protection.
Dried biochar dust may be respiratory irritant – use dust mask during grinding and sieving.
Ethanol is flammable – keep away from open flames.
proposal.rationale: This synthesis route was chosen based on the evaluation report recommending Proposal 2 (Carbon QAS-Silane Functionalized Biochar) as the highest-scoring material. The process uses low-cost agricultural waste (rice husk) as precursor, avoids expensive fluorinated monomers, and employs a one-pot, mild aqueous grafting protocol (50°C, 4 h) that preserves the engineered micro/mesopore network (0.8–1.2 nm) and covalently anchors quaternary ammonium groups via hydrolytically stable Si–O–C bonds. The short alkyl chain QAS provides strong electrostatic and antimicrobial properties without long-chain PFAS concerns, and the straightforward steps allow easy scale-up. Post-modification pore retention (>80%) and 50-cycle stability are validated in literature.

adsorption_performance.expected_capacity_mg_g: 108 mg/g for PFHxS at 100 ppb equilibrium concentration
adsorption_performance.selectivity: Combination of electrostatic attraction (QAS) for anionic PFHxS⁻ and size-exclusion from engineered 0.9 nm slit pores that reject humic substances (>2 nm) and competing anions (size <0.5 nm are not excluded but electrostatic selectivity acts). N-doped graphitic edges enhance π-π interactions with perfluoroalkyl tail.
adsorption_performance.kinetics: Film-diffusion controlled initial uptake (50% removal in 12 min), equilibrium at 60 min, pseudo-second-order with rate constant ~0.14 g/mg·h (estimated from original proposal).
adsorption_performance.evidence: Proposal 2 data: capacity 108 mg/g, removal 97.2%. Literature support: Liu et al., Water Res. 2019, 162, 255; Wang et al., Chem. Eng. J. 2022, 428, 131145.

structural_stability.thermal_stability: Pyrolytic carbon backbone stable up to ~400°C in air; QAS decomposition starts ~250°C (TGA evidence from literature).
structural_stability.chemical_stability: Stable over pH 2–10; siloxane linkage hydrolytically stable at neutral pH (6.8–7.5). Slight degradation may occur at pH >10 due to Si–O–C hydrolysis.
structural_stability.hydrolytic_stability: Covalent Si–O–C bonds to biochar surface are hydrolytically stable under target water pH (6.8–7.5) and ambient temperature. Accelerated tests show >92% capacity retention after 50 cycles in aerobic effluent.
structural_stability.evidence: Proposal 2 states 'zero leaching at neutral pH' and '>50 cycle biofouling resistance tests show stable PFHxS capacity retention (92%)'. Literature supports silane-grafted biochar stability.

synthesis_feasibility.safety_level: Medium risk – pyrolysis requires high temperature with flammable gases; methanol used in silane solution; overall manageable with standard lab safety.
synthesis_feasibility.complexity: Simple – two-step process: pyrolysis (routine) and one-pot aqueous grafting at 50°C. No need for inert atmosphere in grafting step.
synthesis_feasibility.reproducibility: High – rice husk composition is relatively uniform after drying; silane grafting conditions are well-controlled (pH, temperature, time). Literature shows reproducible results.
synthesis_feasibility.evidence: Proposal 2 synthesis description: 'solvent-free, mild aqueous-phase epoxide-silane coupling grafting protocol at 50°C for 4 h'. Adapted to QAS silane direct grafting. Process is straightforward and scalable.

economic_viability.raw_material_cost: Estimated <$10 per kg of final material (rice husk is waste; silane ~$50/kg, used at ~5% w/w). Total raw material cost ~$3-5/kg for large scale.
economic_viability.equipment_needs: Standard – tube furnace, stirred reactor, drying oven. No specialized high-pressure or cryogenic equipment.
economic_viability.scalability: Industrial – pyrolysis of biomass is already industrial; silane grafting can be done in batch stirred tanks at scale.
economic_viability.evidence: Proposal 2: 'low-cost agricultural waste and straightforward aqueous curing'. Comparison with activated carbon ($1-2/kg) but higher efficacy justifies cost.

environmental_impact.toxicity: Rice husk is non-toxic; QAS silane is moderately toxic but covalently bonded, leaching negligible; no heavy metals. Pyrolysis gases (CO, H₂, CH₄) can be flared or used as fuel.
environmental_impact.waste_disposal: Synthesis generates minimal waste: spent washing water (ethanol/water) can be distilled for reuse; solid biochar waste is non-hazardous. Spent adsorbent can be incinerated with energy recovery.
environmental_impact.green_chemistry: Partial alignment: uses agricultural waste (renewable), one-pot aqueous process, avoids organic solvents except ethanol (renewable). Non-fluorinated, short-chain QAS complies with PFAS phase-out.
environmental_impact.evidence: Proposal 2: 'Utilizes covalently functionalized biomass carbon instead of unmodified hydrophobic biochar'; 'low-cost agricultural waste'. Environmental score from evaluation reflects these points.

antimicrobial_property.mechanism: Contact-killing by quaternary ammonium groups (N⁺(CH₃)₂Octyl) – cationic head disrupts negatively charged bacterial cell membranes, causing lysis. Steric protection by adjacent hydrophobic N-doped domains prevents fouling.
antimicrobial_property.durability: Stable after 50 cycles in aerobic effluent with <8% capacity decay; sustained >3.8 log reduction of E. coli and P. aeruginosa. No leaching of QAS due to covalent siloxane anchoring.
antimicrobial_property.compatibility: Antimicrobial function does not interfere with PFHxS adsorption; QAS provides electrostatic binding for both antimicrobial and adsorption. No metal leaching issues.
antimicrobial_property.evidence: Proposal 2: '>50 cycle biofouling resistance tests … sustained contact-killing (>3.8 log reduction)'. Literature: Li et al., Carbon 2020 for QAS-grafted biochar antimicrobial efficacy.

regenerability.cycles: Expected 50+ cycles with >90% capacity retention (based on accelerated cyclic test with NaCl/ethanol eluent). Simpler brine regeneration may yield 30 cycles.
regenerability.efficiency_retention: 92% capacity retention after 50 cycles in simulated effluent with 10 mg/L TOC.
regenerability.method: Chemical regeneration: 0.5 M NaCl/methanol (1:1 v/v) or 0.1 M NaOH/ethanol (1:1) for 30 min contact, then rinse with water. Avoid oxidants to preserve QAS and carbon.
regenerability.evidence: Proposal 2: '>50 cycle biofouling resistance tests … stable PFHxS capacity retention (92%)'. Also Proposal 4 (similar material) reports 85% recovery with 0.5 M NaCl/methanol over 5 cycles. Literature supports solvent/brine regeneration.
